content.problemStatement:
For a permutation PPP of length NNN, we define L(P)L(P)L(P) to be the length of the longest increasing subsequence in PPP. That is, L(P)L(P)L(P) is the largest integer KKK such that there exist indices i1<i2<…<iKi_1 \lt i_2 \lt \ldots \lt i_Ki1​<i2​<…<iK​ such that Pi1<Pi2<…<PiKP_{i_1} \lt P_{i_2} \lt \ldots \lt P_{i_K}Pi1​​<Pi2​​<…<PiK​​.

Define PRP^RPR to be the permutation (PN,PN−1,…,P1)(P_N, P_{N-1}, \ldots, P_1)(PN​,PN−1​,…,P1​).

You are given a positive integer NNN. You need to output a permutation PPP of length NNN such that L(P)=L(PR)L(P) = L(P^R)L(P)=L(PR), or say that none exist.

**Note:** PPP is said to be a permutation of length NNN if PPP is a sequence of length NNN consisting of NNN **distinct** integers between 111 and NNN. For example, (3,1,2)(3, 1, 2)(3,1,2) is a permutation of length 333, but (1,4,2)(1, 4, 2)(1,4,2), (2,2,3)(2, 2, 3)(2,2,3) and (2,1)(2, 1)(2,1) are not.


Input Format

The first line of input contains a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
Each test case consists of a single line containing one integer NNN — the length of the permutation to be constructed.


Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line `"YES"` if there exists a valid permutation, and `"NO"` if there doesn't. If you outputted `"YES"`, on the next line, output a valid permutation PPP as NNN space-separated integers, the ithi^{th}ith of which is PiP_iPi​.


You can print each letter of the string in any case (upper or lower) (for instance, strings `YES`, `yEs`, and `yes` will be considered identical).
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 2·10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a single integer N (1 ≤ N ≤ 2·10^5).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a line with "YES" if a required permutation exists, otherwise "NO". If the answer is "YES", output a second line containing N distinct integers – a permutation of {1,…,N} – such that the length of its longest increasing subsequence equals the length of the longest decreasing subsequence (which is the LIS of its reverse).

sampleTestCases.explanation: For N=2 no permutation has LIS = LDS, so answer is NO. For N=3 we use k=2, blocks [1,2] and [3]; output 2 1 3. LIS=2 (1,3) and LDS=2 (2,1).

N=1 trivially works. N=4 uses k=2 giving blocks (1,2) and (3,4) reversed inside each block → 2 1 4 3. N=5 uses k=3 → blocks (1,2,3) and (4,5) reversed → 3 2 1 5 4. Both have LIS = LDS = 2 for N=4 and =3 for N=5.

solution.approach: For a permutation P, LIS(P^R) equals the length of the longest decreasing subsequence (LDS) of P. Hence we need a permutation with LIS = LDS. By the Erdős‑Szekeres theorem, for any integer k, a permutation of length N ≤ k·k can be built with LIS = LDS = k. Choose k = ceil(sqrt(N)). If N = 2, k = 2 but a permutation of length 2 cannot have both LIS and LDS equal to 2, so the answer is NO. For all other N, construct the permutation by dividing the numbers 1…N into consecutive blocks of size at most k. Inside each block output the numbers in decreasing order; the blocks themselves are in increasing order because they contain larger numbers. This yields LIS = number of blocks = k and LDS = size of the largest block = k, satisfying the requirement. The construction runs in O(N) per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, O(ΣN) overall (≤ 2·10^5).
